refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure and report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined productivity target. The latter is a business outcome dependent on operational execution, workforce skill, and external factors beyond system design scope. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—to provide accurate, timely throughput data—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself is an acceptance criterion applied to the measured data, not a functional requirement of the system.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct instrumented trials during representative operations. Collect independent ground-truth measurements of material throughput (e.g., via weighbridge, volume survey, or third-party audit) and compare against system-reported values. Verify measurement accuracy, latency, and availability across the evaluation period. Confirm that reported data is accessible to Site Manager and Safety Manager stakeholders in agreed formats and intervals.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: Acquire real-time operational data (cycle time, load mass/volume, equipment position, operational state) from the NGHE equipment.
FR-NGHE-001.2: Calculate cumulative material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired operational data.
FR-NGHE-001.3: Store and retrieve historical throughput records with timestamp and operational context.
FR-NGHE-001.4: Communicate throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Tele-operator) in human-readable and machine-readable formats.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input or output) over time and derive throughput from level-delta and cross-sectional geometry
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + spot-check mass sampling) and reconcile via statistical fusion to report throughput with confidence bounds
DP5: Instrument material transport mechanism (conveyor speed, pump displacement, bucket fill cycles) and calculate throughput from mechanism kinematics and material properties
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Sensor selection (mass vs. volumetric) is decoupled from flow-rate acquisition logic; choice depends on material type and acceptance criteria, not on the DP itself
Integration method (numerical summation, time-weighted averaging) is independent of sensing modality
Reporting format (units, time windows, confidence intervals) is a separate concern from measurement acquisition
Calibration and drift compensation are orthogonal to the core DP and can be managed as a separate control loop
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low coupling because the logical concept—continuous instantaneous measurement integrated over time—does not prescribe a specific technology or force dependencies between measurement, integration, and reporting. Alternative sensing approaches (load cells, Coriolis meters, ultrasonic level, etc.) can all satisfy this DP without redesign. The DP is solution-neutral and supports modular realization.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material properties (density, particle size distribution) are stable or known during evaluation period. Risk: Significant variation in density or moisture content could invalidate mass-to-volume conversions; mitigation: specify material acceptance criteria or use dual-modality sensing.

Assumption: Continuous sensing is feasible and cost-effective for the material type and worksite environment. Risk: Abrasive, sticky, or highly variable materials may degrade sensor accuracy or require frequent maintenance; mitigation: conduct pre-deployment sensor durability trials.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient fluctuations and sensor noise. Risk: Short evaluation windows may amplify measurement uncertainty; mitigation: define minimum evaluation duration based on process dynamics and sensor noise floor.

Assumption: Sensor drift and calibration drift are negligible or can be corrected in real time. Risk: Uncorrected drift over extended operations could systematically bias reported throughput; mitigation: implement periodic in-situ calibration checks or redundant verification.

Assumption: Acceptance criterion `productivity ≥ productivity_target` is deterministic. Risk: If target is not clearly quantified or if measurement uncertainty is large, pass/fail determination may be ambiguous; mitigation: define target with explicit tolerance band and measurement uncertainty budget.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan permits installation and operation of continuous sensing equipment without introducing new hazards. Risk: Sensor placement, power supply, or data transmission could conflict with safety protocols; mitigation: coordinate sensor design with safety review early.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) whether throughput is measured in mass or volume (or both), (2) the required measurement uncertainty or confidence level (e.g., ±5% at 95% confidence), (3) the minimum evaluation period and expected process variability, and (4) any material-specific constraints (abrasiveness, temperature, moisture) that may influence sensor selection. Clarify whether `productivity_target` is a single point value or a range, and define the decision rule for acceptance (e.g., mean throughput over period ≥ target, or 90th percentile ≥ target). This will enable tighter DP-to-IM mapping and reduce risk of measurement ambiguity during validation.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Physical and logical boundary encompassing all onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition systems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and cumulative throughput computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) via telemetry channel

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period parameters, sensor calibration commands, and flow measurement mode directives from external tasking authority

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses network link for bidirectional communication of sensor data and control signals if remote sensing or distributed computation is required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: continuous sensing hardware, signal acquisition, integration logic, and cumulative computation are all vehicle-resident subsystems. Diffusion limited to telemetry export of results and tasking input of parameters. This is the natural physical and functional home for real-time flow measurement.

Poor containment: NGHE Solution is a higher-level orchestration/planning layer. Placing continuous sensing and real-time integration here creates tight coupling to vehicle hardware, violates separation of concerns, and forces all sensor data through a non-real-time system. High diffusion of sensing logic across vehicle and NGHE boundary.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a remote command/monitoring interface, not a sensing or computation platform. Placing flow rate acquisition here creates architectural inversion (remote system controlling local sensors) and introduces latency/reliability issues. Sensing hardware remains on vehicle; logic split across two domains.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a cross-cutting concern for hazard monitoring and alert generation. Flow rate measurement is a functional capability, not a safety service. Placing it here conflates measurement with safety logic and creates diffusion across vehicle sensors, safety evaluation, and alert generation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1 because: (1) continuous sensing hardware (flow sensors, mass/volume transducers) is physically resident on the vehicle; (2) real-time signal conditioning and integration computation must execute with minimal latency on vehicle-local processors; (3) cumulative throughput state is maintained as vehicle-local data; (4) external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume the results via Telemetry interface, not participate in measurement. This achieves high cohesion (all measurement and integration logic co-located) and low coupling (clean export of results via standard telemetry).
containment_risks: Risk: If vehicle lacks onboard computational capacity for real-time integration, DP may require offloading to NGHE Solution, degrading containment. Mitigation: ensure vehicle has sufficient edge compute or revise DP to defer integration to NGHE (see recommended_revision).

Risk: If sensor calibration or evaluation period parameters are dynamically managed by NGHE Solution, tight coupling via Tasking interface may emerge. Mitigation: design Tasking interface as a clean contract (parameter passing only, no state sharing).

Risk: If Safety Services requires direct access to raw flow rate data for anomaly detection, diffusion across Vehicle and Safety Services boundary may increase. Mitigation: Safety Services consumes integrated results via Telemetry; raw sensor data remains internal to Vehicle.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP1 is well-matched to Vehicle containment as stated. No revision needed. However, if vehicle computational constraints are severe, consider alternative DP: 'DP1-alt: Vehicle acquires instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing and streams raw sensor data to NGHE Solution; NGHE Solution performs integration over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput.' This would shift integration logic to NGHE Solution (higher-level, non-real-time) and reduce vehicle-side computation, at the cost of increased network traffic and latency. Recommend DP1 (Vehicle-resident integration) for real-time throughput tracking; DP1-alt for post-hoc analysis.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and sufficiently addresses FR: continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) with integration over evaluation period logically solves the requirement to measure and report throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time. The DP is technology-neutral, measurable, and does not over-constrain implementation choices.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk (low): Telemetry export of results creates a DP<->IM bridge; Vehicle module must serialize and transmit instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput. Mitigation: define Telemetry interface contract (data format, frequency, latency bounds) to prevent tight coupling.

Diffusion risk (low): Tasking input of evaluation period parameters and sensor calibration commands creates a DP<->IM bridge. If NGHE Solution dynamically modifies these parameters without Vehicle-side validation, state coherence may degrade. Mitigation: Tasking interface must be stateless parameter-passing; Vehicle retains authority over sensor calibration state.

Diffusion risk (medium): If vehicle computational capacity is insufficient for real-time integration, DP logic may be split between Vehicle (sensing) and NGHE Solution (integration), violating cohesion. Mitigation: validate vehicle edge compute capacity early; if inadequate, adopt DP1-alt (defer integration to NGHE).

Diffusion risk (low): Safety Services may request direct access to raw sensor data for anomaly detection, creating an undeclared DP<->IM bridge. Mitigation: enforce that Safety Services consumes only integrated results via Telemetry; raw sensor data remains Vehicle-internal.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module achieves excellent containment (0.92 score): continuous sensing hardware, signal conditioning, integration logic, and cumulative throughput computation are all vehicle-resident. Diffusion is minimal and limited to clean external interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link bidirectional). NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are correctly excluded from measurement logic ownership; they consume results only. This allocation respects physical locality (sensors on vehicle), real-time constraints (integration on vehicle-local processors), and separation of concerns (measurement vs. orchestration vs. remote command vs. safety).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over agreed evaluation period' is directly verifiable via Vehicle-resident cumulative throughput computation exported via Telemetry. Measurement can be validated against ground truth (manual sampling, calibrated reference sensors) during representative operations. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that do not impede measurement capability; Vehicle module must comply with safety interlocks (e.g., flow measurement only when material stream is active and safe).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Vehicle is the unambiguous owner of DP1 realization. Functional responsibility (measure and report throughput) maps to a single, cohesive module boundary with well-defined external interfaces. No ambiguity regarding which system owns sensing hardware, integration computation, or cumulative state. Tasking and Telemetry interfaces establish clean contracts for parameter input and result export.

recommended_revisions: Validate vehicle edge compute capacity for real-time integration of flow rate data over the agreed evaluation period. If capacity is insufficient, adopt DP1-alt: Vehicle streams raw sensor data to NGHE Solution; NGHE Solution performs integration and computes cumulative throughput. Document the trade-off (latency vs. vehicle compute load).

Define Telemetry interface contract: specify data format (instantaneous flow rate, cumulative throughput, timestamp, sensor status), transmission frequency (e.g., 1 Hz for instantaneous, end-of-period for cumulative), and latency bounds. Ensure NGHE Solution and Teleoperation consume results only; no back-channel sensor control.

Define Tasking interface contract: specify parameter schema (evaluation period start/end, sensor calibration coefficients, flow measurement mode). Ensure Vehicle validates and acknowledges parameter receipt; NGHE Solution does not assume state synchronization.

Document sensor calibration procedure and ownership: Vehicle or external authority? If external (NGHE Solution), define calibration command protocol and validation logic to prevent stale or conflicting calibration state.

Specify raw sensor data retention policy: does Vehicle log raw flow rate samples for post-hoc analysis, or only export integrated results? If logging is required, define storage capacity and lifecycle (e.g., circular buffer, archival to NGHE Solution).

Confirm that Safety Services accesses flow rate data only via Telemetry (integrated results); no direct Vehicle sensor access. If Safety Services requires raw data for anomaly detection, create a separate DP and IM boundary for safety-specific sensing.

decision.justification: FR1 (Measure and report throughput) passes all three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) with high containment (0.92) and clear allocation to Vehicle module. However, the gate results expose critical dependencies and diffusion risks that must be resolved through sub-level decomposition before implementation can proceed safely. Specifically: (1) Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts are identified as essential but not yet designed—these are DP<->IM bridges requiring explicit functional requirements; (2) Vehicle edge compute capacity validation is flagged as a prerequisite decision point with a fallback DP1-alt, indicating that the current DP assumes a constraint that has not been verified; (3) Safety Services access to flow data creates an undeclared DP<->IM bridge that must be formalized as a separate functional requirement; (4) Sensor calibration ownership and state coherence are unresolved, creating a potential safety-critical gap. These are not minor implementation details—they are architectural decisions that affect system safety, real-time behavior, and interface contracts. Decomposing FR1 into sub-FRs for (a) real-time flow integration, (b) Telemetry export, (c) Tasking input handling, and (d) Safety Services data access will expose and resolve these dependencies before detailed design. Given high safety criticality, this decomposition is mandatory.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Perform real-time integration of instantaneous flow rate measurements to compute cumulative throughput over the evaluation period, with computational latency and accuracy bounds compatible with vehicle edge compute capacity.

FR1.2: Export instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput results via Telemetry interface with defined data format, transmission frequency, and latency bounds, ensuring NGHE Solution and Teleoperation consume results only.

FR1.3: Accept and validate evaluation period parameters (start/end time, evaluation duration) and sensor calibration coefficients via Tasking interface, with Vehicle-side state coherence and acknowledgment protocol.
FR1.4: Provide flow rate data (raw or integrated) to Safety Services for anomaly detection and safety interlocks, with clear ownership of safety-critical thresholds and decision authority.

FR1.5: Maintain sensor calibration state and procedure (ownership, update protocol, validation logic) to ensure measurement accuracy and prevent stale or conflicting calibration across Vehicle and NGHE Solution.

revise.why: DP1 is logically sound but under-specified regarding the architectural boundaries and interface contracts that the gate analysis identified as critical. Before proceeding to sub-level DP design, clarify: (a) Does DP1 assume Vehicle-resident integration (real-time, on-edge), or is DP1-alt (deferred integration to NGHE Solution) a viable alternative pending compute capacity validation? (b) What is the Telemetry interface contract (data schema, frequency, latency)? (c) What is the Tasking interface contract (parameter schema, validation, acknowledgment)? (d) Is sensor calibration a Vehicle-owned or NGHE-owned function, and what is the state coherence protocol? These clarifications will inform sub-level DP design and prevent diffusion risks from propagating into implementation.

assumptions: Vehicle edge compute capacity is sufficient for real-time integration of flow rate data; if not, DP1-alt (deferred integration) is acceptable pending NGHE Solution latency analysis.
Telemetry export is a clean, read-only interface; NGHE Solution and Teleoperation do not back-channel sensor control commands.
Tasking input is stateless parameter-passing; Vehicle retains authority over sensor calibration state and validates all incoming parameters.
Safety Services accesses flow rate data only via Telemetry (integrated results); no direct Vehicle sensor access is permitted.
Sensor calibration is a Vehicle-owned function with optional NGHE Solution input; Vehicle validates and acknowledges calibration updates.
Raw sensor data retention (logging, archival) is a separate concern from real-time integration; policy to be defined at sub-level decomposition.
open_risks: Vehicle edge compute capacity may be insufficient for real-time integration; if so, DP1-alt (deferred integration to NGHE Solution) introduces latency and network dependency, creating a new failure mode (network loss -> loss of throughput measurement). Mitigation: validate compute capacity early; if inadequate, define fallback DP1-alt with latency bounds and network resilience strategy.

Telemetry interface contract is not yet defined; if data format, frequency, or latency are not specified, NGHE Solution and Teleoperation may make incompatible assumptions, causing integration failures. Mitigation: define Telemetry interface contract as a sub-level DP (FR1.2).

Tasking interface contract is not yet defined; if parameter schema and validation logic are not specified, Vehicle and NGHE Solution may have conflicting state, causing measurement errors or safety violations. Mitigation: define Tasking interface contract as a sub-level DP (FR1.3).

Safety Services access to flow data is undeclared; if Safety Services requires raw sensor data for anomaly detection, a separate DP and IM boundary must be created to avoid tight coupling with Vehicle measurement logic. Mitigation: formalize Safety Services data access as a sub-level FR (FR1.4).

Sensor calibration ownership and state coherence are unresolved; if calibration is split between Vehicle and NGHE Solution without a clear protocol, measurement accuracy may degrade or safety interlocks may fail. Mitigation: define sensor calibration ownership and state coherence protocol as a sub-level DP (FR1.5).

Raw sensor data retention policy is undefined; if Vehicle logs raw flow rate samples without a defined lifecycle (circular buffer, archival, deletion), storage capacity may be exceeded or data may be lost. Mitigation: define raw sensor data retention policy at sub-level decomposition.
evidence_needed: Vehicle edge compute capacity specification: CPU, memory, real-time OS, integration algorithm complexity (floating-point operations per sample, latency budget). Validate that real-time integration is feasible; if not, quantify latency and network dependency of DP1-alt.

Telemetry interface specification: data schema (JSON, protobuf, CAN), transmission frequency (Hz), latency bounds (ms), error handling (loss, corruption, out-of-order). Validate that NGHE Solution and Teleoperation can consume results within latency bounds.

Tasking interface specification: parameter schema (evaluation period, sensor calibration coefficients), validation logic (range checks, type checks), acknowledgment protocol (Vehicle confirms receipt and state update). Validate that Vehicle and NGHE Solution can synchronize state without race conditions.

Safety Services data access specification: which data (raw or integrated), access frequency, latency bounds, decision authority (Vehicle or Safety Services). Validate that safety interlocks are not compromised by data access patterns.

Sensor calibration procedure: ownership (Vehicle or NGHE Solution), update protocol (command format, validation, acknowledgment), state coherence (how Vehicle and NGHE Solution maintain consistent calibration state). Validate that calibration updates do not introduce measurement errors or safety violations.

Raw sensor data retention policy: logging requirement (yes/no), storage capacity (GB), lifecycle (circular buffer, archival, deletion), access control (Vehicle-internal or exported). Validate that storage capacity is sufficient and data lifecycle does not conflict with safety or operational requirements.

Ground truth validation: manual sampling or calibrated reference sensors to verify Vehicle-reported throughput against independent measurement during representative operations. Validate measurement accuracy and sensor calibration procedure.
